coredata.dc:title: Stable GQD@PANi nanocomposites based on benzenoid structure for enhanced specific capacitance

coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Jianli
Zhou, Yaya
Xiong, Zhiyuan
Guo, Guizheng
Sun, Youyi
Li, Dan
Liu, Yaqing
coredata.dc:description: Here, a stable PANi doped with graphene quantum dot (GQD) was synthesized by a new synthesis process for application in electrode materials of supercapacitors. Furthermore, the effect of GQD content on structure and electrochemical properties of PANi was investigated in detail. It showed excellent specific capacitance (3632.0 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1) as electrode of supercapacitor for GQD@PANi nanocomposites with 10.0 wt%. The result was attributed to PANi with more benzenoid structure, which provided more electrochemical active sites and reduced internal resistance under assistant with GQD. The findings open a new path to design and prepare high-performance electrode materials based on PANi materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
PANi
Benzenoid
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of PANi doped with graphene quantum dot (GQD@PANi)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectrum, (B) TEM image, (C) the size distribution and (D) XRD of GQDs, the inset of (A) and (B) is the optical photograph of GQD solution under UV lamp (365 nm) and high-resolution TEM image of an individual GQD, respectively.

(A) Optical photograph of (a) pure PANi DMF solution, (b) GQD@PANi DMF solution, (c) GQD@PANi DMF solution after centrifugation under UV lamp (365.0 nm). Optical photograph of GQD@PANi powders with various contents of (d) 0, (e) 5.0 wt%, (f) 10.0 wt% and (g) 20.0 wt%. (B) UV–vis spectra of GQD@PANi solution with various contents of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c) 10.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt%.
(A) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs, FT-IR spectra of GQD@PANi with different content of (B) 0, (C) 5.0 wt%, (D) 10.0. wt% and (E) 20.0 wt%.
(A) Full scan XPS spectra of (a) GQD@PANi and (b) GQDs. The C1s spectrum of (B) GQDs and (C) GQD@PANi. (D) The N1s spectrum of GQD@PANi.
SEM of GQD@PANi with various content of (A) 0, (B) 5.0 wt%, (C) 100 wt% and (D) 20.0 wt%.
The EDS spectra of (A) GQD@PANi (10.0 wt%) and (B) pure PANi. The inset of (A) and (B) is the elemental mapping images of GQD@PANi(10.0 wt%) and pure PANi, respectively.
(A) Nitrogen adsorption isotherm at 77.0 K and (B) pore size distribution of GQD@PANi with various loading.

(A) The CV curves of GQD@PANi with various contents of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c) 10.0 wt% and (d) 200 wt%. The CV curves of GQD@PANi with various content of (A) 0, (B) 5.0 wt%, (C) 10.0 wt% and (D) 20.0 wt% at various scan rates of (a) 20.0 mV s−1, (b) 40.0 mV s−1, (c) 60.0 mV s−1, (d) 80.0 mV s−1 and (e) 100.0 mV s−1.

(A) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the GQD@PANi with various doping content of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c)10.0.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt% at a current density of 1.0 A g−1. (B) galvanostatic charge/discharge curve of the GO@PANi with doping content of 10.0 wt% at a current density of 1.0 A g−1. (C) galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the GQD@PANi with doping content of 20 wt% at various current densities of (a)1.0 A g−1, (b)2.0 A g−1 and (c)4.0 A g−1, (D) the capacitor of the GQD@PANi with various doping content of (a) 0, (b) 5 wt%, (c)10.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt% depended on current densities and (E) the capacitor of the GQD@PANi with various doping content of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c)10.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt% depended on tested cycles.
(A)IR and (B) XRD spectra of GQD@PANi (10.0 wt%) (a) before and (b) after 1000 cycling tests, (C)SEM image of GQD@PANi (10.0 wt%) after 1000 cycling tests.

(A)Nyquist plots of GQD@PANi with various contents of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c)10.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt%, (B) Bode Plots of GQD@PANi with various contents of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c)10.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt%, (C) Bode-phase of GQD@PANi with various contents of (a) 0, (b) 5.0 wt%, (c)10.0 wt% and (d) 20.0 wt%, (D) Nyquist plots of GQD@PANi (10.0 wt%) (a) before and (b) after 1000 cycling tests.
Synthesis process of the GQD@PANi and its chemical structure.
The ion diffusion mechanism of (A) pure PANi and (B) GQDs@PANi, (C) electron transfer mechanism of GQDs@PANi.
The composition of GQD@PANi with various contents of GQDs.
The area of GQD@PANi with various contents of GQDs.
The CV results of GQD@PANi with various contents of GQDs.

row.entry: Shift of anodic Redox reaction peak (mV)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparison in electrochemical performance of GQD@PANi with other electrode materials based on PANi.

head.ce:title: Stable GQD@PANi nanocomposites based on benzenoid structure for enhanced specific capacitance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria 3010, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a stable PANi doped with graphene quantum dot (GQD) was synthesized by a new synthesis process for application in electrode materials of supercapacitors. Furthermore, the effect of GQD content on structure and electrochemical properties of PANi was investigated in detail. It showed excellent specific capacitance (3632.0 F g−1 at 1.0 A g−1) as electrode of supercapacitor for GQD@PANi nanocomposites with 10.0 wt%. The result was attributed to PANi with more benzenoid structure, which provided more electrochemical active sites and reduced internal resistance under assistant with GQD. The findings open a new path to design and prepare high-performance electrode materials based on PANi materials.

ce:section.ce:para:
Conducting polymers have received great research interest in the fields of nanoscience and nanotechnology due to their high conductivity and redox behavior [1,2]. They were widely used for variety of applications such as light emitting display devices, energy conversion, energy storage, catalyst and sensors [3–6]. Especially, polyaniline (PANi) as a well-studied pseudocapacitive material has been widely investigated as the electrode of supercapacitors due to be low-cost aniline monomers, simple synthesis, environmental stability, relatively high conductivity and the high electrochemical activity associated with multiple redox states [7,8]. However, up to now, after nearly two decades of research, PANi-based electrode has not yet been commercialized. This is fundamentally due to the inherent compromise between electrochemical active surface area and internal resistance, restricting the improvement of specific capacitance [9]. In principle, the reversible redox couple
− NH
/
HN
· +
reaction provides the electrochemical charge storage during galvanostatic charge process [10]. The electrochemically generated charge is released during galvanostatic discharge process, resulting in producing electrical current [10]. Unfortunately, in most cases, the PANi generated by the most methods were half-oxidized state and doped with other acids to improve the electrical conductivity of PANi [11–13]. Although it enhanced electrical conductivity of PANi, but it also leaded to the decrease of electrochemical active surface area in PANi due to present more N groups and the interaction between dopants and NH of PANi [14]. From this perspective, to achieve high specific capacitance, the inevitable route is to maximize the electrochemical active surface area while enhancing the electrical conductivity of PANi.

Recently, carbon materials (eg. graphene, carbon nanotube, g-C3N4 and graphene quantum dot) were usually used to modify the PANi because they did not only provide the electrochemical active sites, but also could effectively improve the conductivity of electrode material, reducing internal resistance of the electrode material [15–21]. However, in most cases, the PANi was still half-oxidized state. Furthermore, the PANi and carbon materials were physically mixed, in which the heterogeneous contact and interface between PANi and carbon materials was not benefit to transfer to surface of PANi for electrochemically generated charge [18,22]. To this end, developing a soluble PANi doped with graphene quantum dot as molecule dopant is indispensable to achieve high specific capacitance.

Herein, a PANi with more benzenoid structure was synthesized to improve the stable properties of PANi in solution. Furthermore, the PANi with lots of NH groups provided more electrochemical active surface area. At the same time, the GQD as molecule dopant could be introduced to PANi by the chemical interaction. The GQD did not only provide the double-layer properties, but also enhancing charge transfer to surface of PANi. It showed higher specific capacitance comparing to other electrode materials based on PANi reported in previous works. These results represent a new, simple and highly effective approach to develop better electrode material based on PANi for application in electrochemical energy storage in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PANi doped with graphene quantum dot (GQD@PANi)
ce:section.ce:para: The GQD@PANi was synthesized by the following two steps as shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the GQD was synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method. First, GO was prepared using Hummer's method and then 500.0 mg of GO was finely dispersed in 50 ml DMF by stirring and ultrasonication for 30.0 min. Then, the solution was transferred into 80.0 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and it was heated to 180.0 °C for 8 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered. The brown filter solution was collected, which was the GQDs suspension in DMF. Secondly, 200.0 mg aniline was added into 20.0 mL water with different amounts of above GQDs under stirring for 30.0 min. 500.0 mg ammonium per-sulfate (APS) was dissolved in 20.0 mL mixing solution (VDMF/Vwater = 1:1). Then the above APS solution was added dropwise into GQD and aniline mixing solution after setting the temperature at 5.0 °C and the mixture could stand overnight at that temperature. The desired product was dried and washed repeatedly first by water and then by methanol to remove APS and oligoaniline. Finally, the product was dried under vacuum at 60.0 °C for 1 days to collect a solid mass of the GQD@PANi. The loading of GQD was calculated by the addition mass of GQD and mass of collect products. For a comparison, the pure PANi and GO/PANi was also prepared by the similar synthesis process.

The morphology and size of products were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, JSM-6701F) with energy-dispersive spectra and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010) at an acceleration voltage of 200.0 kV.
X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on a Rigaku Dmax-r C X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) operated at 40.0 kV and 100.0 mA.
UV absorption spectra were obtained on a cary 5000 spectrophotometer.
Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) study was performed using KBr pellets on a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA).
The specific surface areas were measured using the BET method by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77.0 K using an automatic surface are and size analyzer (3H-2000PS2).
A Keithley 2400 source meter was used to measure the electrical resistance.

Electrochemical measurements, including cyclic voltammetry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (CV, CHI 660D workstation) and galvanostatic charge/discharge curves (NEWARE BTS battery tester) were carried out in a three-electrode electrochemical cell using an Hg/HgCl (1 M KCl filled) electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and the GQD@PANi/NF as the working electrode with 6.0 M KOH as the electrolyte.

The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was calculated for cyclic voltammetry curve by following equation [23]:
E C S A =
S H
/ V
0.21
(
C · c
m
− 2
)
· M
where S
H represents the area of CV curve (A · V), V indicates the scan rate (mV s−1) and M is the mass of active material (g/cm2).

The specific capacitance was calculated for galvanostatic charge/discharge curve by following equation [12]:
C =
I Δ t
/
(
m Δ V
)
where C represents the specific capacitance (F g−1) or volumetric capacitance F (cm−3), m indicates the mass of active material (g), and ΔV is the applied potential window (V), I is the charge or discharge current, Δt is the time required for a full discharge.

In this work, we used DMF as a sole solvent in a one-step solvothermal process to produce GQDs. Here, the big GO was decomposed thermally to small GO at 180 °C [24]. At the same time, during the reaction, DMF acted as the solvent as well as a weak reduction agent [24]. The big GO was split and reduced simultaneously, and thus the GQDs were obtained. The formation of the dispersible GQDs was confirmed by the UV–vis spectrum, TEM images and XRD spectrum as shown in Fig. 1 . In UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1A), the peak at 242.0 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains of the GQDs, while the band at 301.0 nm was due to the n-π* transition of the carboxylic acid functional groups present in the GQDs [25]. DMF dispersed GQD solution produced sky-blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (365.0 nm) as shown in inset of Fig. 1A. TEM images exhibited homogenous GQDs with a size of 5.0–13.0 nm (7.9 nm average), and a narrow distribution of almost 86.0% concentrated in 7.0–10.0 nm as shown in Fig. 1B and C, respectively. The inset of Fig. 1B displayed a representative high-resolution TEM image of an individual GQD. The distinct crystal lattice indicated the crystallinity of GQD and the lattice parameter of 0.24 nm represented the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [26]. The XRD pattern of the GQDs was further characterized as shown in Fig. 1D. The sample showed a wide (002) peak centred at 21.2° (around 0.34 nm), which was consistent with the lattice spacing of graphite [26]. All these observations indicated the formation of GQDs with carboxylic acid functional groups.

Here, the GQDs as molecule dopant could be uniformly doped in PANi due to the DMF solution and reaction temperature of 5.0 °C. Firstly, the PANi showed more benzenoid structure, resulting in the formation of more NH groups. It was benefit to easily form interaction between CO groups of GQD and NH groups of PANi as shown in Scheme 1. Secondly, the NH groups provided good soluble properties of PANi in DMF/water mixing solution, which restricted aggregation and phasing out of PANi [27,28]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the pure PANi and GQD@PANi was dispersed in DMF solution, which remained high stability at room temperature without precipitation for at least two months. Furthermore, the pure PANi was difficult to be separated from the DMF/water mixing solution by a centrifugation method. It was attributed to that the pure PANi was high dispersion and stability in solution. In a contrast, the GQD@PANi could be separated entirely from the mixing suspension solution by a centrifugation method, and the suspension solution after centrifugation did not produce sky-blue fluorescence under a UV lamp as shown in Fig. 2A, too. It is worth pointing out that neither the mechanical mixture of GQDs nor PANi responds to a centrifugation, PANI and GQDs will remain in suspension. The result demonstrated the formation of GQD@PANi nanocomposites. At the same time, it also indicated that the stable properties of GQD@PANi decreased comparing to pure PANi. Fig. 2A also shows the optical photograph of pure PANi and GQD@PANi. It clearly showed uniformly yellow color for all samples, which was attributed to PANi with more benzenoid units [10,29]. These results confirmed the formation of GQD@PANi with more benzenoid structure. Fig. 2B represents the UV–Vis spectra of the GQD@PANi with various contents. All samples clearly showed one absorption peak around 380.0 nm, which was assigned to the ð-ð* transition of the benzenoid rings [15]. It was interesting to note that the ð-ð* transition of the benzenoid rings of PANi showed a red or blue shift after combing with GQDs. These results further confirmed the formation of PANi with more benzenoid rings, which formed chemical interaction with GQDs.

Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectra of GQD@PANi with various contents. The absorption peaks at 1666.0 cm−1 and 1501.0 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CC of GQDs, respectively [30]. The absorption band at 1104.0 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CO of GQDs [30]. These results further indicated the formation of GQDs with carboxylic acid-containing groups, which made it highly dispersible in DMF and water. As shown in Fig. 4 B, the strong absorption band at 3486.0 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH [6]. The peaks at 1589.0 cm−1 and 1515.0 cm−1 were ascribed to CC of quinonoid and benzenoid units, respectively. The CC peak intensity of benzenoid units was obviously higher than that of quinonoid units. The peak at 1293.0 cm−1 was ascribed to the CN stretching of the secondary aromatic amine [6]. These results further confirmed the formation of PANi with more benzenoid units. In the spectra of GQD@PANi (Fig. 4C–E), almost all FT-IR peaks of the GQDs and PANi were maintained. At the same time, the absorption bands assigned to NH and CC of benzenoid units exhibited large blue shift comparing to pure PANi. In contrast, the absorption band assigned to CC of quinonoid units was slight shift. Furthermore, there presented a new strong peak at 1110.0 cm−1 for the polaron formation of the benzenoid structure (BN+HB) due to doping with GQD [31]. Furthermore, the benzenoid rings contents of different samples were calculated by the its IR absorption peak area as shown in Table 1 . In Table 1, ratios of different benzenoid and quinonoid rings species are based on the equation: BX% = B/(B + Q) × 100%, where B and Q represented peak areas of benzenoid and quinonoid rings, which were both acquired from CC peak of IR spectra. Benzenoid rings content of GQD@PANi was slight change after introduction of GQDs. Notably, all samples showed high contents of benzenoid rings. At the same time, the content of N+H was also calculated by the its IR absorption peak area as shown in Table 1. Ratios of different NH and N+H species were based on the equation: NX% = N1/(N1+N2) × 100%, where N1 and N2 represented peak areas of NH and N+H, respectively. It clearly showed that N1 decreased with increasing in content of GQDs. It was well-known that it was an important N species for electrocatalysis of PANi. N+H could be the most important N species to promote onset potential of PANi because of localized lone electron pairs in N-type species [32]. Those results did not only suggest the formation of GQD@PANi with more benzenoid units, but also indicated the presence of N+H by the interactions between CO group of GQDs and NH of PANi.

The chemical structure of GQD@PANi was further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra as shown in Fig. 4. Fig. 4A clearly showed two sharp peaks at 285.5 eV and 533.8 eV, corresponding to the C and O elements of GQDs, respectively [4]. Excluding the peaks of C and O elements, a new peak at 401.4 eV was also observed as shown in Fig. 4A(a), which was originated from N element of PANi. These results indicated the formation of GQD@PANi. In addition to this, the measured C1s spectrum of GQDs could be fitted with three components, such as 284.6 eV (sp2 bonded carbon), 285.6 eV (sp3 bonded carbon) and 288.7 eV (CO group) as shown in Fig. 4B. The result further confirmed the surface components of the GQDs obtained with COO group. The C1s spectrum of GQD@PANi also clearly showed similar three components of 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV and 291.1 eV as shown in Fig. 4C, corresponding to the sp2 bonded carbon, the sp3 bonded carbon and CO group of GQDs, respectively [33]. At the same time, excluding three components of GQDs, there presented others two new components located at 284.1 eV and 286.0 eV, which were indexed to the sp2 bonded carbon and the sp3 bonded carbon PANi, respectively [33]. Thus, the XPS peaks of N1s were reasonably composed of three Gaussian-Lorentzian peaks with the binding energy of approximate 400.1 eV, 401.8 eV and 402.2 eV as shown in Fig. 4D. The peak with the lowest binding energy (400.1 eV) was attributed to the amine-like nitrogen atoms (NH) [34]. The peak centred at 401.8 eV is attributed to the cationic nitrogen atoms
(
N · +
)
on the polymer backbone compensated with the GQD and the highest binding energy (402.2 eV) peak is due to the protonated amine units [35]. Here, the amine-like nitrogen atoms were the dominant component. In a contrast, the peak with binding energy (ca.398.7 eV) assigned to the imine-like (N–) structure was not observed. The result further confirmed that the PANi had more benzenoid structure. Ratio of NH and N+H species was calculated to be 71.0% and 28.0% by the its XPS peak area, which was almost consistent with the result of IR. In addition, the component peak 288.7 eV assigned to CO group were weak and shift, and the 400.1 eV assigned to NH was shift compared with the result reported in previous works [4,15,30]. These results confirmed the formation of chemical interaction between CO of GQDs and NH of PANi as shown in Scheme 1.

Fig. 5 shows the FE-SEM images of the GQD@PANi with different contents. It clearly showed the PANi polymer film with rough surfaces. There were few particles to be observed, which was different from previous works [17,27,28,36]. It was attributed to that the PANi with good dispersion easily formed polymer film. In contrast, these GQD@PANi obviously showed spherical nanoparticles with scale-like surfaces as shown in Fig. 5B–D. It was attributed to that the PANi chain could easily form self-assemble aggregate due to be decrease of dispersion and stability in solution, resulting from the interaction between GQD and PANi. The result was consistent with previous results [17,27,28,36], in which the rod, cube or fiber PANi was formed by the self-assemble method. All GQD@PANi samples showed similar mace-like morphology. These results indicated that the PANi easily formed self-assemble aggregate due to be the interaction between NH of PANi and CO of GQDs.

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) of GQD@PANi and pure PANi was also characterized as shown in Fig. 6 A and B, respectively. It clearly presented C and N elements for all samples. In addition to this, excluding the peaks assigned C and N element, a new peak centered at 0.51 K eV appeared for GQD@PANi, corresponding to the O element of GQD as shown in Fig. 6A [37]. According to the wight ratio of C, N and O element, the content of GQD was calculated to be about 11.6 wt% for GQD@PANi, which was almost consistent with GQDs content (10.0 wt%) of GQD@PANi measured by the wight method. At the same time, no peaks corresponding to other elements were detected. The elemental mapping images of GQD@PANi and pure PANi were characterized and compared as shown in inset of Fig. 6A and B, respectively. As seen from Fig. 6A, it clearly revealed N, O and C elements to be uniform distribution in the GQD@PANi. Contrarily, it also showed N and C elements, but the O element was not observed for PANi as shown in Fig. 6D. These results further confirmed the formation of GQD@PANi and pure PANi with high purity.

Adsorption isotherm of N2 gas was carried out to characterize the specific active surface area and porous structure of GQD@PANi with various content. Fig. 7 A and B shows the typical adsorption/desorption isotherm and the pore size distribution of GQD@PANi at 77.0 K, respectively. Their isotherms showed similar shape of type IV, indicating to form porous structures mainly composed of mesoporous (according to IUPAC classification) as shown in Fig. 7A [38]. In addition to this, it was found that a hysteresis loop obviously decreased with increasing in content of GQDs. It indicated that there was fewer mesoporous at the higher content of GQDs [39]. The surface area and pore characteristics of pure PANi and GQD@PANi was shown in Table 2 . It could be found from Table 2 that the BET specific surface area and pore volume of the nanocomposites decreased with increasing in content of GQD. The BET specific surface area and pore volume of pure PANi was about 105.7 m2/g and 0.16 cm3/g, respectively. When the GQDs were introduced to the PANi, the BET specific surface area and pore volume of GQD@PANi (20.0 wt%) decreased to be 10.6 m2/g and 0.02 cm3/g, respectively. Furthermore, it was interesting that the present PANi and its nanocomposites showed larger surface area and pore volume comparing to the other materials based on PANi [38–41]. The result was attributed to that the PANi was good dispersion and stability in solution. And then, the introduction of GQDs leaded the aggregate of PANi, which was consistent with the result of SEM images. In contrast, the average pore size slightly increased comparing to the pure PANi. It was attributed to that the pore structure resulted from interval between PANi chains or PANi aggregate. The interval between PANi aggregate was generally larger comparing to PANi chain. These results further indicated the formation of PANi with good dispersion and stability, which provided the larger accessible surface area and speed up the ion diffusion process, indicating the suitability of GQD@PANi as electrode material of supercapacitor.

The electrochemical performance of the electrodes made from GQD@PANi was firstly evaluated and compared by cyclic voltammogram (CV) as shown in Fig. 8 A. A couple of redox peaks at 0.07–0.08 V and 0.30–0.35 V were clearly observed for all the electrodes. The result indicated the faradaic capacitive behaviour of GQD@PANi electrode, in which the redox reaction of
− NH
/
− HN
· +
transitions associated with anions OH− was reversible [8,10]. At the same time, the asymmetric nature of the CV curve suggested the combined double-layer and pseudocapacitive contributions to the total capacitance [42,43]. The large area under the CV curves represented the good capacitance value, in which the CV curve area of GQD@PANi firstly increased and then decreased with increasing in content of GQD [44]. The result indicated that the GQD@PANi with 10.0 wt% GQDs showed highest specific capacitance comparing to other samples. Furthermore, CV currents with one pairs of redox peaks of GQD@PANi increased with increasing in content of GQD, indicating that the conductivity or internal resistance of nanocomposites as the electrode material increased or decreased with increasing in content of GQD [45]. The enhanced conductivity was attributed to the polaron formation of the benzenoid structure and GQD, which provided a pass way of electron. Moreover, the ECSA of all samples was calculated for cyclic voltammetry curve, and the results were concluded in Table 2. The ECSA of GQD@PANi with various content of 0, 5.0 wt%, 10.0 wt% and 20.0 wt% was about 23.5 m2/g, 29.4 m2/g, 31.9 m2/g and 30.3 m2/g, respectively. The larger ECSA of nanocomposites was attributed to the double-layer properties of GQDs comparing to pure PANi. The high-power characteristic of GQD@PANi could be identified from their voltammetric response at various scan rates ranging from 20.0 mV s−1 to 100.0 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 8B–D. It exhibited well-resolved as well as reversible redox peaks for all samples. With increasing scan rate, peak current clearly increased with broadening of CV peaks, signifying the pseudocapacitance feature of GQD@PANi [44]. At the sweep rate of 20 mV s−1, a pair of redox peaks was located at ca. 0.12 V and 0.29 V, respectively. With the scan rate increasing from 20.0 mV s−1 to 100.0 mV s−1, the anodic peaks shifted towards positive potential and the cathodic peaks shifted towards negative potential. At the sweep rate of 100.0 mV s−1, a pair of redox peaks was located at ca. 0.07 V and 0.35 V, respectively. Even at a high scan rate of 100.0 mV s−1, the CV curve still clearly showed a pair of redox peaks, indicating that the GQD@PANi was beneficial to fast charge-discharge due to the high interface area, easy ion diffusion, low resistance and super-fast electronic transport rate [46]. Remarkably, the peak potential shifted only ca. 44.0 mV for a 5-time increase in the scan rate, suggesting that the pure PANi electrode possessed lower polarization [47], resulting from the efficient electron and ion transportation in the porous integrated electrodes of pure PANi comparing to GQD@PANi as shown in Table 3 .

The galvanostatic charge-discharge properties of the GQD@PANi with various contents were performed in a 6.0 M KOH electrolyte between 0.1 V and 0.5 V (vs. Hg/HgCl) as shown in Fig. 9 A. The charge/discharge curves of all samples exhibited the obvious oxidation reduction peaks and nonlinear characteristics, demonstrating pseudocapacitive character. The specific capacitance of GQD@PANi with various content of 0, 5.0 wt%, 10.0 wt% and 20.0 wt% was about 1062.0 F g−1, 2976.0 F g−1, 3662.0 F g−1 and 662.0 F g−1 at current density of 1.0 A g−1, respectively. In a comparison, the charge/discharge curve of GO/PANi with content of 10.0 wt% was also characterized as shown in Fig. 9B, in which the specific capacitance was about 956.8 F g−1 at current density of 1.0 A g−1. Fig. 9C showed the galvanostatic charge-discharge properties of GQD@PANi with content of 10.0 wt% at different discharge current densities. The specific capacitance of GQD@PANi were 3662.0 F g−1, 4440.0 F g−1 and 2552.0 F g−1 at the current densities of 1.0 A g−1, 2.0 A g−1 and 4.0 A g−1, respectively. The specific capacitance retention of the GQD@PANi with various contents was compared at various current densities as shown in Fig. 9D. It showed that the specific capacitance retention of GQD@PANi with various contents of 0, 5.0 wt%, 10.0 wt% and 20.0 wt% was about 137.0%, 113.0%, 70.2% and 93.2%, respectively. The result indicated that the PANi with more benzenoid rings showed better high-rate performance comparing to GQD@PANi and other electrode materials reported in previous works [9,11–14,16–18]. It was due to be lower polarization at high current densities, which was consistent with result of CV [47]. The cycle life of GQD@PANi was further investigated at a current density of 4.0 A g−1 for 1000 cycles as shown in Fig. 9E. As expected, the specific capacitance of GQD@PANi with various contents of 0, 5.0 wt%, 10.0 wt% and 20.0 wt% was still 1065 F g−1, 2278 F g−1, 1980 F g−1 and 469 F g−1 with 73.1%, 67.8%, 77.0% and 74.2% retention. These results indicated that the GQDs and benzenoid rings slightly affected on cycle life of PANi.

By comparing the specific capacitance of the GQD@PANi to that of others electrode materials based on PANi reported in previous works as shown in Table 4 . It was found that the GQD@PANi prepared in present works had the highest value so far among all the reported data. It was well-known that the specific capacitance of electrode materials was closely to the electrochemical activity, BET surface area and the transfer rate of electrons [48]. Based on the above experimental results, a mechanism for the improvement of specific capacitance was proposed as shown in following. Firstly, the PANi with more benzenoid units was polymer chain state and more NH groups, which could offer a large active surface area and more active sites to be accessed by electrolyte, resulting in a high utilization of active materials as shown in Scheme 2 . Secondly, the introduction of GQDs leaded to be double-layer contributions and form
− N
H · +
species by the chemical interaction between CO of GQDs and NH of PANi, which could offer a large electrochemical active surface area and enhance electron transfer in PANi chain as shown in Table 2. So, although there was lower BET surface area, there was larger specific capacitance for GQD@PANi comparing to pure PANi.

The electrochemical stability of the GQD@PANi (10.0 wt%) was important factor from the viewpoint of practical applications and have been evaluated through 1000 cycles experiments. The similar XRD pattern and IR spectrum of GQD@PANi was identical before and after 1000 consecutive cycles as shown in Fig. 10 A and B, respectively [6]. This result indicated that the GQDs were adsorbed on the surface of PANi. SEM measurements also showed a similar morphology for GQD@PANi before and after 1000 consecutive cycles as shown in Figs. 5C and 10C, respectively. These results indicated that the phase structure and morphology of GQD@PANi was slight change after 1000 consecutive cycles. These results confirmed the good stability of the GQD@PANi electrode materials.

To further study the mechanism of GQD@PANi with high specific capacitance, the EIS measurement was conducted as shown in Fig. 11 . Nyquist Plots of all samples were firstly characterized as shown in Fig. 11A. The impedance plot consisted of a semicircle in the high-to-medium frequency region arising from the chemical reaction process and followed by a line at the low frequency region which was corresponding to ion diffusion in the electrode material [54]. At low frequencies, the straight lines were nearly perpendicular to the relaxes for all samples, indicating excellent capacitive behaviour [55]. At high frequency (close to 100 kHz), the equivalent series resistance (ESR) obtained from the first intersection with the real axis was about 0.6 Ω (0), 0.59 Ω (5.0 wt%), 0.56 Ω (10.0 wt%) and 0.54 Ω (20.0 wt%). The result indicated a low internal resistance for all GQD@PANi electrodes, which was also confirmed by the high electrical conductivity of GQD@PANi as shown in Table 3. Moreover, the vertical line in the high-to-medium frequency region for all sample indicated that there was almost not any charge-transfer resistance for the ion diffusion, which was obvious lower that reported in previous works [11–13,16–18]. It suggested that all sample obviously showed higher charge transfer rate comparing to the previous works. Bode Plots of all samples were shown in Fig. 11B. They showed similar Bode-Plot curves. The impedance of the PANi was obviously reduced by the introduction of GQD. Furthermore, The impedance of GQD@PANi firstly increased and then decreased with increasing in content of GQDs. When the impedance of the PANi came down from 3 × 103 Ω to 5 × 102 Ω on addition of 10 wt% GQD. Fig. 11C shows the Bode phase of all samples. The phase angle of all samples was very close to −80° for frequencies up to 3.0 Hz suggesting the behaves of supercapacitor device based on PANi or GQD@PANi as an ideal capacitor at low frequencies [56]. On the other hand, the phase angle (−74.4° to −77.6°) of the supercapacitor based on GQD@PANi was lower than that (−72.1°) of supercapacitor based on PANi, suggesting that the GQD@PANi based supercapacitor behaves more like an ideal capacitor than the PANi based supercapacitor. The results were attributed to that PANi with more benzenoid units enhanced the wettability of the electrode and improved electrolyte ionic accessibility. And the relative hydrophility was generally believed to be favourable to reduce charge transfer resistance between electrolyte and electrode material [55]. At the same time reasons, the pure PANi and GQD@PANi also showed high rate-performance. Furthermore, Nyquist Plots of GQD@PANi before and after cycling experiments were conducted and compared as shown in Fig. 11D. It also showed slight change. The result further confirmed the structure stability under electrochemical process.

In this work, a stable PANi doped with GQDs was controlled synthesis by a facile method. And the loading of GQDs was easily controlled by adjusting the concentration of GQDs aqueous solution. Furthermore, the solution properties and dispersion of GQD@PANi were determined by the loading of GQDs, in which they decreased with increasing in loading of GQDs. Moreover, the electrochemical properties of GQD@PANi strongly depended on its solution properties and dispersion. The GQD@PANi (10.0 wt%) exhibited highest specific capacitance of 3632.0 F g−1 for application in electrode of supercapacitors. The good electrochemical performance might be closely related to the intrinsic characteristics of PANi with more active sites and
H
N · +
, resulting from more benzenoid units and GQDs as dopant comparing to other electrode materials based on PANi. Therefore, this provided a new and facile method to simultaneously enhance electrochemical active sites and charge transfer in the PANi for preparation of high energy electrochemical capacitors based on PANi materials in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants (51773184), and the Shanxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (201701D121046).
